text: BALLYMACWILLIAM, a parish 4 m. NW of Edenderry, King's co. Area 4,976 acres. Pop. 1,193.
BALLYMAGARVEY, a parish 6 m. S of Slane, co. Meath. Area 915 acres. Pop. 104.
BALLYMAGAURAN, a hamlet in the p. of Templeport, co. Cavan, 4 m. NE of Ballinamore. Pop. in 1831, 89.
BALLYMAGIBBON, a hamlet in the p. of Cong, co. Mayo, 21 m. NE of Cong.
BALLYMAGLASSON, a parish 4 m. S by W of Dunshaughlin, co. Meath. Area 3,476 acres. Pop. 567.
BALLYMAGOOLY, a village in the p. of Rahan, co. Cork. Pop. in 1841, 212.
BALLYMAGORRY. See BALLINAGORRY.

BALLYMAHON, a market and post-town, partly in the parish of Noughaval, but chiefly in the parish of Shruel, co. Longford, 10 m. NW by N of Athlone, 10 m. SE of Lanesborough, and 53 m. W by N of Dublin. Its site is the skirt of a gently sloping hill along the Inny, about 3 m. above the point of that river's inosculation with Lough Ree. It consists principally of one very spacious street. Its public buildings are a church with a steeple, a spacious Roman Catholic chapel, an indifferent old markethouse, and a handsome bridge of five lofty and wellconstructed arches. The Inny is navigable from B. to Lough Ree; and the Royal canal passes near it in the line extending from Dublin to Tarmonbarry. m. ENE of its confluence with that river, 62 m. The current trade consists of the interchange of yarns, cloths, groceries, and miscellaneous articles for the produce of the field and the dairy. A series of small manufacture is conducted in linen frieze, linseywoolsey, leather, shoes, nails, and gingerbread.—B. is said to have derived its name from the circumstance of Mahon, king of Thomond, the predecessor and elder brother of the celebrated Brian Boromh, having, in 960, obtained, in its vicinity, a victory over Feargal, the son of Ruarc, king of Connaught. Goldsmith the poet was educated in B. by Mr. Hughes, vicar of Shruel, amd afterwards had much connection with the town and some of its inhabitants. "His description of the alehouse," says a writer, long and minutely acquainted with the locality, "may be considered as applicable to most of the habitations of the farmers in the neighbourhood of Lissoy and Ballymahon, where until the late introduction of modern expensive habits, every parlourfloor was sanded or flagged, had 'its bed by night, a chest of drawers by day,' and exhibited either on a chimney-board, or in an open cupboard, a parcel of broken or unbroken pieces of china, glass, or stained earthenware; while the walls were covered with gunracks, fishing-tackle, and homely prints, among which latter ornaments, 'the twelve good rules, and royal game of goose,' always obtained a conspicuous place." Pop. in 1831, 1,081; in 1841, 1,229. Houses 197.
BALLYMAKENNY, a parish 23 m. N of Drogheda, co. Louth. Area 1,581 acres. Pop. 499.
BALLYMANNY, a parish 44 m. E of Kildare, co. Kildare. Area 507 acres. Pop. 193.
BALLYMARTIN, a parish in co. Antrim, It consists of a main body and a detached portion. Area of the Upper section, 2,421 acres; of the Lower section, 385. Pop. of the whole 870.
BALLYMARTLE, a parish 4 m. N by E of Kinsale, co. Cork. Area 5,503 acres.
BALLYMASCANLON, a parish 2 m. NE of Dundalk, in co. Louth. Area 15,997 acres. Pop. 6,674.

BALLYMENA, a market and post-town in the p. of Kirkinriola, co. Antrim, on the Braid or Broad river, 7 m. N by E of Randalstown, 2 m. above the confluence of the Braid and the Main. The general appearance of B. is that of an antiquated and irregular town in the process of transmutation into modern neatness of masonry, and combined spaciousness and order in street arrangement. The principal street is new, wide, and well built. In its vicinity are 14 bleach-greens; and in the town itself a flax spinning-mill and a distillery. The linen trade is carried on extensively and with spirit; and sales in brown linen alone are said to average in amount £70,000 a-year. Pop. 5,152; including the suburb of Henryville, 5,549.
BALLYMITTY, a parish 4 m. S by W of Taghmon, co. Wexford. Area 1,365 acres. Pop. 393.
BALLYMODAN, or Ballymoodan, a parish in co. Cork, containing about three-fourths of the borough of Bandon, and also the v. of Roundhill. Area 8,090 acres. Pop. 9,298.
BALLYMOE, a village in the p. of Drimtemple, in co. Galway, 4 m. S by E of Castlerea, on the r. bank of the Suck. Pop. 300.

BALLYMONEY, a parish, partly in co. Londonderry, but chiefly in co. Antrim. The Dunluce section contains the town of Ballymoney, and the v. of Stranocum. Area 22,676 acres. Pop. 11,727.—Also a post and market-town, in the Dunluce section of the above parish, on a small tributary of the Bann, 3 of Coleraine. It lies dispersedly over an area of about \( \frac{3}{4} \) of a m. in length. Pop. 2,490.
BALLYMONEY, or Crossenhare, a parish 734 m. SW by W of Bandon, co. Cork. It contains the village of Ballinean. Area 7,308 acres. Pop. 3,733.
BALLYMORE, a parish 21 m. W of Broadway, co. Wexford. Area 2,525 acres. Pop. 568.

BALLYMORE, or Ballymore-Eustace, a parish, containing a town of the same name, formerly in co. Dublin, now in co. Kildare. Area 4,203 acres. Pop. 2,129.—The town is pleasantly situated on the Liffey, 31 m. SW of Blessington. A very handsome bridge here carries the highway across the Liffey.

BALLYMORE, or St. Owen's of Loughseudy, a parish, containing the greater part of a town of the same name, in co. Westmeath. Area 10,465 acres. Pop. 3,487.—The market and post-town of B., chiefly in the above parish, and partly in that of Killare, stands on the road between Athlone and Mullingar, 12 m. WSW of Mullingar. Pop. 574. It is a long, irregular, ill-built town, remarkable only for some historical associations, and for its horse and cattle fairs.
BALLYMORE, or TANDERAGEE, a parish in co. Armagh. It contains the town of Tanderagee, the villages of Acton and Clare, and part of the town of Poyntzpass. Area 14,158 acres. Pop. 11,505.
BALLYMOREEN, or BALLYMARREEN, a parish 4 m. SE by S of Thurles, co. Tipperary. Area 3,846 acres. Pop. 1,203.

BALLYMOTE, a small town in the p. of Emlyfadd, co. Sligo, 2 m. E of the river Owenmore, 12 m. NW of Boyle. B.-castle, though a ruin, is very regular, and both handsome and strong. It forms a square upon a line of 150 ft.; 60 ft. high; and is flanked and quoined by towers 6 ft. thick in the wall, with a strong rampart and parapet all round. It was built in 1300 by Richard de Bourg, second Earl of Ulster. Pop. 839.

BALLYMOYER, or BALLYMYRE, a parish 25 m. NE of Newtown-Hamilton, co. Armagh. Area 7,381 acres. Pop. 3,071. The linen manufacture is everywhere diffused throughout this p.; and substantial woollen fabrics are made for domestic use.
BALLYMURPHY, a village in the p. of St. Mullins, co. Carlow. Pop. 158.

BALLYMURTAGH, a metalliferous mountain in the p. of Castle-Macadam, co. Wicklow. It is situated near the first "meeting of the waters," 41 m. S of Rathdrum. It has an alt. of about 1,000 ft. above sea-level, and abounds in rich copper-ore. About the middle of last century, copper-mines in the mountain were remuneratingly worked. In 1787, they were rented by the company of miners, who originally discovered the celebrated Parys mine in Anglesey; subsequently they were leased to the Hibernian Mining company; and now, and for a long series of years, they have been worked with success. In their vicinity are the copper-mines of Cronbane and other localities; and these, jointly with the mines of Ballymurtagh, are the most extensive of their class in Ireland. BALLYN- For all names commencing thus, see
BALLINBALLYORGAN, a village in the p. of Kilflyn, co. Limerick. Pop. 334.

BALLYOUGHTERA, a parish in the vicinity of Castlemartyr, co. Cork. Area 4,532 acres. Pop. 1,728. Ballyoughtera literally signifies 'a town of braziers;' yet there is no copper-ore near this place, but iron-mines are almost everywhere round it.

BALLYOVEY, a parish 61 m. NNW of Ballinrobe, co. Mayo. Area 27,622 acres; of which 6,602 acres are in Lough Mask, 1,177 acres in Lough Carra, and 313 acres in other small lakes. Pop 4,505. BAL The surface extends along the W side of Loughs Carra and Mask, forms the peninsula between them, and ascends the frontier glens, bogs, and mountains of Joyce-Country. Glenmask mountain on the S boundary, and Slieve-Bohaan on the W boundary, have altitudes of respectively 1,418 and 1,294 ft.
BALLYPHILIP, a parish, containing the town of Portaferry, in co. Down. Area 2,330 acres. Pop. 3,086.
BALLYPOREEN, a village in the p. of Templetenny, co. Tipperary, 3 m. SW of Clogheen. Pop. 772.
BALLYQUIN, a village in the p. of Cloghane, co. Kerry. Pop. 193.

BALLYQUINTIN, a headland in the p. of Witter, co. Down, forming the southern extremity of the peninsula of Little Ardes, and screening the NE side of the entrance of the strait leading up to Lough Strangford. The passage nearly opposite the headland is only about half an Irish mile wide, and is scoured by a tidal current of 6 knots an hour in both flood and ebb. Near the headland, and occasioned by a rock called the Ranting-wheel, is a kind of whirlpool, very dangerous to boats. Two lighthouses overlook the entrance of the strait, the one at Portaferry, South Rock, and the other at Ardglass.

BALLYRAGGET, or Donaghmore, a parish, containing the town of B., in co. Kilkenny. Area 5,270 acres. Pop. 2,694. The highest ground has an alt. of 879 ft.—The town of B. stands on the Nore, 4½ m. SW by W of Castlecomer. Pop. 1,577.

BALLYRAINE, a creek at the head of Lough Swilly, co. Donegal, Ulster. It runs up within a mile of Letterkenny, and forms the harbour of that town, and of some extent of circumjacent country. In 1835, the exports from it were estimated in value at £20,834.

BALLYROAN, a parish, containing a village of the same name, in Queen's co., Leinster. Area 9,682 acres. Pop. 3,689. Cullinagh mountain, on the E border, has an alt. of 1,045 ft.; and a hill in the S has an alt. of 623 ft. The village is 41 m. N of Ballinakill. The Kilkenny line of railway passes within 2 m.
BALLYROOSKY, a village in the p. of Clondavadock, co. Donegal. Pop. 284.

BALLYSADERE, or Ballasodare, a parish, containing a town of the same name, in co. Sligo. Area 16,024 acres. Pop. 7,822. The highest ground is near the centre, and has an alt. of 602 ft. The Owenbeg, swollen into considerable volume by the confluence of the Arrow, leaps and tumbles over a long series of precipices and shelving rocks of various heights into the head of Ardnaglass harbour, and forms a most magnificent chain of cataracts. 'The town of the two cataracts,' aptly designates a seat of population on this superb piece of water scenery, and is the literal meaning of the name Ballysadere. A conspicuous feature in the seaward landscape, is the isolated and singularly outlined hill of Knocknara, at the entrance of Ardnaglass harbour, alt. 1,078 ft. -The v. or small town of B. stands at the head of Ardnaglass harbour, 3 m. SW of Sligo. Within a distance of 500 yards close to this village there are a succession of falls in this river amounting to 75 ft. These falls afford six sites for water-power mills on each side of the river.
BALLYSAX, a parish 23 m. SW of Kilcullenbridge, co. Kildare. Area 7,207 acres. Pop. 1,220.
BALLYSCADANE, a parish in co. Limerick, 8 m. SW of Tipperary. It contains the village of Garryspellane. Area 944 acres. Pop. 749.

BALLYSCULLION, a parish, partly in co. Antrim, but chiefly in co. Londonderry, 4 m. NE of Magherafelt. The Londonderry section contains the village of Bellaghy. Area 10,271 acres, exclusive of 2,479 acres of water and islands.
BALLYSCULLOGUE, or Scullogestown, a parish 5 m. WSW of Kilcock, co. Kildare. Area 2,468 acres. Pop. 453.
BALLYSEEDY, a parish 44 m. SE of Tralee, co. Kerry. Area 3,489 acres. Pop. 1,472.

BALLYSHANNON, a post, market, and seaport town, and formerly a parliamentary borough, in the parishes of Kilbarron and Innismacsaint, co. Donegal. It stands on both sides of the Erne, immediately above that river's influx into Ballyshannon harbour, 4 m. WNW of Belleek, 11 m. SSW of Donegal, and 96 m. W by S of Belfast. The harbour is a creek of Donegal bay, about 600 yards long, and 350 yards broad, and looks right through the centre of the entrance of the bay into the open Atlantic. It is greatly exposed to W winds and to the tumultuous roll from the whole sweep of the ocean; and has only about 10 ft. of water in spring-tides. The southern or Innismacsaint section of the town, called the Purt, is aggregately a wretched suburb, and consists principally of an irregular street of poor dwellings, half-a-mile in length, running close to the river, and parallel with it to the head of the harbour. The N or Kilbarron side, or Ballyshannon proper, climbs the sides and crowns the summit of a hill. B. is favourably situated for trade; it occupies the position of the capital of a considerable extent of rich agricultural country; stands at the junction with the sea of a waterline which descends from a great distance inland, has very large lacustrine expansions, and communicates by still water navigation with a great portion of the north of Ireland; and it overlooks the grand seapath to America. But a cataract at the mouth of the Erne, which dissevers the navigation of the sea from that of the Fermanagh lakes, and especially the shallowness, the bars, and the open exposure of Ballyshannon harbour, are stubborn obstacles to prosperity. In 1835, the exports from the town consisted of 10,764 quarters of oats, valued at £11,130; and the imports consisted of coals, iron, stones, slate, oakbark, meal, and flour, sugar, tea, glass, earthenware, and other articles, and were valued at £9,524. With the exception of brewing, distilling, and salt-making, and of the ordinary operations of handicraft, the whole trade consists in the exchange of miscellaneous wares for agricultural produce. The salmon-fishery at the town lets for £1,000 a-year; and its produce is sent to the Liverpool and London markets. Area of the town 352 acres; of which 186 acres are in the Kilbarron section. Pop. in 1831, 3,775; in 1841, 4,307. Houses 597.
BALLYSHANNON, or Ballysonnan, a parish 41 m. SW of Kilcullen-bridge, co. Kildare. Area 2,815 acres. Pop. 494.
BALLYSHEEHAN, a parish 33 m. N of Cashel, co. Tipperary. Area 9,216 acres. Pop. 3,068.
BALLYSHONBOY, or KILQUANE, a parish, partly in co. Cork, but chiefly in co. Limerick. Area of the Cork section 426 acres; of the Limerick section 2,350 acres. Pop. of the whole 934.

BALLYSPELLIN, a celebrated spa in the parish of Fartagh, 14 m. E of Johnstown, co. Kilkenny. It has long been in high repute, and is a favourite resort of invalids. "The spring," says the author of the Statistical Survey of the County, "rises out of a rock of brittle slate, consisting of ferruginous argilite. The hill immediately above is formed of the same materials. To the taste the water appears slightly acidulous, and when first taken up and held to the light, it is full of a number of minute bubbles, which soon rise and vanish." Its effects are said to resemble those of the waters of Pyrmont, and the Groisbeck spring at Spa.
BALLYSPILLANE, a parish 81 m. SE by S of Rathcormack, co. Cork. Area 2,088 acres. Pop. 603. | Dee-bridge, and 275 in the v. of Lauriston. BAL
BALLYSUMAGHAN, a parish in co. Sligo, 41 m. SW of Dromahaire. Area 4,217 acres. Pop. 1,902.
BALLYTANKARD. See TANKARDSTOWN.
BALLYTARSNEY, a parish in co. Kilkenny, 61 m. SE by E of Carrick-on-Suir. Area 896 acres. Pop. 258.

BALLYTEIGUE, or BALLINTEAGUE, a bay in co. Wexford, between Hook lighthouse on the W, and Ballyteigue-head on the E. It measures about 14 m. across the entrance, and indents the land, nearly in the form of the segment of a circle, to the extent of about 4 m. The Little and the Great Saltee islands lie from 23 to 5 m. S of the E side of its entrance; and various islets and rocks lie between these islands and the mainland.
BALLYTIVNAN, a village in the parish of Calry, co. Sligo. Pop. 127.
BALLYTOBIN, or BALLINTOBIN, a parish 4 m. SE of Callan, co. Kilkenny. Area 2,394 acres. Pop. 714.

BALLYTORE, a small town in the parish of Timolin, co. Kildare, 5 m. NNE of Castle-Dermot, in a fertile valley watered by the rivulet Griese. The town is remarkable for neatness, cleanliness, and order. Quakers are its chief inhabitants, and carry on several branches of manufacture. Edmund Burke received here the early part of his education under the celebrated Quaker Abraham Sheckelton.
BALLYTRAIN, or BELLATRAIN, a village in the parish of Aughnamullen, co. Monaghan. Pop. 197.
BALLYTROLEEN, a village in the parish of Ballinaboy, co. Cork. Pop. 107.
BALLYTRUSTIN, a parish in Little Ardes, 11 m. SE of Portaferry, co. Down. Area 1,681 acres. Pop. 754.
BALLYVALDEN, a parish 4 m. ESE of Oulart, co. Wexford. It contains part of the village of Blackwater. Area 3,911 acres. Pop. 1,550.
BALLYVALEW, or BALLYVALLOO, a parish 6 m. NNE of Wexford, co. Wexford. Area 1,891 acres. Pop. 822.
BALLYVARY, a village in the parish of Kilda comogue, co. Mayo, 6 m. NE of Castlebar. Pop. 116.
BALLYVAUGHAN, a fishing-village in the parish of Dromcreehy, co. Clare, at the head of Ballyvaughan bay, 41 m. ESE of Black Head promontory.
BALLYVINNY, or KILLASPIGMULLANE, a parish 4 m. S by W of Rathcormack, co. Cork. Area 1,852 acres. Pop. 569.
BALLYVOR, or Ballivor, a village in the parish of Killaconnigan, co. Meath, 6 m. W by S of Trim. Pop. 158.

BALLYVOURNEY, a parish, containing a village of the same name, in co. Cork. Area 26,603 acres. Pop. 4,466. The v. of B. is romantically situated on an eminence which overhangs the S bank of the Sullane, 75 m. W of Macroom.
BALLYWALTER, or WHITECHURCH, a parish, containing a village of the same name, in co. Down. Area 3,378 acres. Pop. 1,916. The village is situated on the coast, 3 m. E by N of Greyabbey. Pop. 743.
BALLYWILLIN, or MILTOWN, a parish, partly in co. Antrim, and partly in co. Londonderry, 32 m. NNE of Coleraine. The Antrim section contains the v. of Portrush. Area 4,673 acres. Pop. 2,202.
BALMACLELLAN, a parish in Kirkcudbrightshire, stretching along the Urr, which forms its E boundary. Pop. in 1801, 554; in 1841, 1,134, of whom 113 were in the v. of B., 11 m. NE of New Galloway.
BALMAGHIE, a parish in Kirkcudbrightshire, stretching along the water of Dee. Pop. in 1801, 969; in 1841, 1,252, of whom 243 were in the v. of

BALME, or Belma, a commune of Piedmont, in the prov. and 28 m. NW of Turin, on one of the head-streams of the Stura, and at the W base of the Cottian Alps. It is remarkable for, and derives its name from, a grotto, vernacularly balme, containing a chapel dedicated to Notre-Dame-de-la-Balme.

BALME (COL DE), a gorge of the Pennine Alps, at the base of the mountain of the same name, extending from the source of the Arve, in Valais, to the valley of Chamouni in Savoy. It forms one of the principal passes of the Alps, and at its highest alt. of 3,000 ft. above the subjacent valley of Chamouni, and 6,400 ft. above sea-level, commands a magnificent view of Mont Blanc on the W, which is seen in profile from its summit to its base, and of the adjacent aiguilles and valleys. To the N of the Col, is the glacier of Trient, and to the SW that of La Tour.
BALME (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Isere, and cant. of Crimieu, 23 m. NNW of La Tour-du-Pin, near the Rhone. Pop. 644.
BALMECEDA, or VALMECEDA, a town of Spain, in Biscay, romantically situated on the banks of the Salcedon, on the high road from Castro to Burgos, at the foot of the Sierra d'Orduna. Pop. 1,384.
BALMERINO, a parish in Fifeshire, stretching along the frith of Tay. Area 3,346 acres. Pop. in 1841, 993, of whom 62 were in the v. of B.

BALMORAL, a picturesque locality in the Highlands of Aberdeenshire, 55 m. from Aberdeen, and 22 m. from Aviemore, recently selected as a private summer-residence for the Queen of England. The noble river and the linn of Dee, and the mountains Lochnagar and Ben Macdui are in the immediate neighbourhood.
BALMUCCIA, a town of Piedmont, 5 m. W of Varallo, and 25 m. NNE of Ivrea, on the l. bank of the Sesia.
BALNAHUAIGH, an islet of Argyleshire, in the p. of Jura, lying midway between Lunga and Eisdale. It is a slate rock about 1 m. in circumf.
BALNE, a township in the p. of Snaith, W. R. of Yorkshire, 41 m. SW of Snaith. Area 2,870 acres. Pop. 341.
BALNOT-SUR-LAIGNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aube, and cant. of Les Riceys. It affords excellent red wine; that of the Vaux is reckoned the finest produced in the dep. Pop. 530.
BALOOCHISTAN. See Beluchistan.
BALOUDION. See BALUDIN.

BALQUHIDDER, a large parish in Perthshire, upwards of 20 m. in length from NE to SW. The writer of the first statistical account of this p. claims the south part of Ben More as in this parish, and estimates its height at 3,903 ft. above sea-level; also the western side of Ben Voirlich, to which he assigns an alt. of 3,300 ft. More recent admeasurements estimate the alt. of Ben More at 3,819 ft.; and Ben Voirlich at 3,180 ft. A little to the S of Ben More is Binean, or 'the Mountain of Birds,' which has a nearly equal elevation. To the SW of Binean is Ben Chroan, or Stuich a Chroan, 3,171 ft.; and to the SE of Ben Chroan are Stobdune and Ben Choan. All these are very lofty mountains; but we have not admeasurements of their respective heights. The principal roads which intersect this parish are that from Callander, by Loch Lubnaig, to Lochearnhead, and through Glen Ogle to Tyndrum; and that from Lochearnhead to Balquhidder. Glen Ogle is a narrow pass hemmed in for several miles on both sides by very lofty and precipitous rocks. Glen Ample is a narrow deep ravine on the eastern skirts of the parish, intersected by a rapid mountain-torrent called the Ample, which flows N into Loch Earn. The vale of Balquhidder, and its two fine and Mudge. BAL lochs, present some very beautiful scenery, and is rife with traditions of Rob Roy, many of whose exploits were performed here; and whose ashes rest in the little churchyard of Balquhidder. This village is 12 m. distant from Callander. Pop. of the parish, in 1801,1,377; in 1831, 1,049; in 1841, 871. Besides the v. of B., this parish contains the village of Lochearnhead, pop. 46; and of Strathyre, pop. 135.
BALRAHAN, or Balrain, a parish in co. Kildare, 3 m. S by W of Maynooth. Area 3,374 acres: Pop. 485.
BALROTHERY, or BALRUDDERY, a parish in co. Dublin. It contains the town and chapelry of Balbriggan, and the village of Balrothery. Area 6,885 acres. Pop. 4,881.
BALS, a river of Norway, in the dio. of Norland, and bail. of Finmark, which falls into the Balsfiord in N lat. 19° 15'.
BALSALL, or TEMPLE-BALSALL, a chapelry in the p. of Hampden-in-Arden, Warwickshire, 10 m. NNW of Warwick. Pop. 1,160.
BALSAM, a lake of Upper Canada, in the dist. of Newcastle, and co. of Durham, to the E of and connected with Sturgeon lake.
BALSANO BAY, an indentation of the N coast of Hayti, in N lat. 19° 40', and W long. 70° 15'.
BALSAPINTADO, or BELSAPENTADO, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Murcia, 10 m. NW of Carthagena.
BALSAR, a town of Quito, in the prov. and 80 m. N of Guayaquil, on the r. bank of the Daule. The adjacent territory is fertile, producing cocoa, sugar, tobacco, and cotton.

BALSAS, a town of Peru, in the dist. of Truxillo, 30 m. WSW of Chachapoyas, on the r. bank of the Maranon.—Also a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Maranham, which rises in the district of Mangabeiras, and running in a generally ENE direction, unites with the Parnaiba in S lat. 7° 10'.—Also a river of Mexico, rising on the eastern confines of the prov. of Mexico, and flowing SSW to about the 18th parallel, where it turns to the W, and crossing the prov. receives near Cayuca the river Zitaquaro on its r. bank, from which point it flows in a SSW direction to the Pacific ocean, which it enters at Zacatula.
BALSCADDAN, a parish in co. Dublin, containing a v. of the same name, 2 m. W by N of Balbriggan. Area 3,948 acres. Pop. 295.
BALSCOTT, a hamlet in the p. of Wroxton, Oxfordshire, 41 m. W by N of Banbury. Area 550 acres. Pop. 199.
BALSHAM, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 9 m. SSE of Cambridge. Area 4,402 acres. Pop. 1,271.
BALSOON, a parish in co. Meath, 5 m. ENE of Trim. Area 1,269 acres. Pop. 340.
BALSORA. See BASSORA.
BALSORANO, a town of Naples, in Abruzzo Ultra 2da, at the foot of Mount Accerella, on the Tibreno river. Pop. 1,200.
BALSOVER. See Bolsover.
BALSTALL, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Solothurn, 9 m. NE of Solothurn. Pop, 800.

BALTA, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Podolia, 21 m. S of Obgopol, on the Kodima river, a tributary of the Bug. Pop. 1,200. - Also an islet of Scotland, lying to the E of the isle of Unst, in the Shetland group, in N lat. 60° 41'. Balta sound, between Balta and Unst, is a bay 2 m. in length, and about half-a-mile broad, so completely shut in by the island of B. that, seen from a distance, it resembles a lake. Both sides of this bay are in a state of high cultivation. The islet is wholly composed of diallage rock, and its cliffs are peculiarly rugged. It formed the N point of the arc of the meridian measured by Roy
BALTERLEY, a township in the p. of Barthomley, in Staffordshire. Area 1,020 acres. Pop. 316.
BALTI. See BULTI.

BALTIC, or Eastern Sea, next to the Mediterranean, the most important of the inland seas of Europe. Though it does not appear that the Baltic was ever visited by the Romans, it is mentioned under various names by several of their writers, as a place, of whose existence, at least, they were perfectly aware. It is the Venedicus Sinus of Ptolemy; the Mare Suevicum of Tacitus; and the Sinus Codanus of Pliny. In the countries which bound it, its ancient name was Variatzkoie More, or 'the sea of Variaghi.' The modern Russians call it Baltiskoè Morè, and the Swedes, Oster Sjow. The length of the Baltic from SW to NE, or from Swinemiinde, at the entrance of the Stettiner-haff to Torneo, is 770 geographical miles: and it is in general about 120 m. broad, but in some places it spreads to the breadth of 150 m., and between Karlscrona and Memel it is 180 m. Its depth, as far as the parallel of Memel, does not exceed 50 fathoms. To the W of Bornholm it does not reach 50 fath.; but in its more northern parts it deepens to 100 and 120 fath. It is of very irregular figure; and comprises an area of about 125,000 geog. sq. m. The Baltic opens from the German sea between 57° and 59° N lat., or the N coast of Jutland, and the S coast of Norway, by a gulf pointing NE, called the Sleeve or Skager-Rack, which extends rather more than 150 m. from the Naze of Norway to the coast of Sweden, and is in average width about 70 m. It next passes several degrees S, in what is called the Cattegat, to the SE of which is the sound of Elsinore, a narrow strait, about 3 m. in breadth, between the coast of Sweden and the island of Zealand. This is the general passage for ships going from the North sea into the Baltic; and a toll is here paid, by way of courtesy, to the crown of Denmark, which, in return, erects lighthouses, and keeps them in proper repair. After passing Zealand, this sea spreads widely to the NE, and at last branches out into the two extensive gulfs of Bothnia and Finland; the former of which stretches N as far as Torneo, within a few degrees of the Arctic circle, 350 m., with a mean breadth of 100 m.; while the latter extends 200 m. in a direction almost due E, till within a short distance of Lake Ladoga, with a mean breadth of 70 m. Both these gulfs are either covered or much impeded with ice during four or five months of the year. History informs us, indeed, that even the whole of the B. has been, at various times, completely frozen over. In 1333, travellers passed on the ice from Lubec to Prussia and Denmark, and tents were erected at certain intervals for their accommodation. The same phenomenon occurred in the years 1399, 1423, 1459, and 1533; in 1709, and 1740, the frosts were also remarkably severe, though the ice was by no means so general or so strong as in the other instances mentioned. These facts serve, with many others, to confirm a favourite theory of modern naturalists,—that the rigour of the seasons in the northern countries of Europe was formerly much greater than at present. Supposed change of level.] Above a hundred years ago the Swedish naturalist Celsius declared his opinion that the level of the waters, both of the B. and the ocean, were suffering a gradual depression. In confirmation of this phenomenon, Celsius appealed to several distinct classes of proofs: 1st, The testimony of the inhabitants on the N shores of the gulf of Bothnia, that towns formerly sea-ports were then far inland, and that the sea was still constantly leaving dry new tracts of land along its borders. 2dly, The testimony of the same inhabitants, that various insulated rocks in the gulf of Bothnia, and on some parts of the eastern shores of Sweden, then rose higher above the level of the sea than they remembered them to have done in their youth. 3dly, That marks had been cut on the fixed rocks on the shore some thirty years or more before, to point out the level at which the waters of the B. formerly stood when not raised by the winds to an unusual height, and that these marks already indicated a sinking of the waters. On the whole, Celsius concluded that the rate of depression amounted to 3 or 4 ft. in a hundred years. To this conclusion it was objected, that there were many parts of the B. where the level of the sea had not fallen, as could be proved by ancient pines and castles standing close to the water's edge, and other natural and artificial monuments. It was further remarked that the new accessions of land were chiefly where rivers entered the sea, and where new sedimentary deposits were forming; and that the marks were not to be depended upon, because the level of the sea fluctuated in consequence of the action of the wind. Von Buch, in the course of his tour in Sweden and Norway, about forty years ago, found at several places on the W shores of Scandinavia, deposits of sand and mud containing numerous shells referable to species now living in the neighbouring ocean. From this circumstance, and from accounts which he received from inhabitants of the coasts of the Bothnian gulf, he inferred that Celsius was correct in regard to a gradual change of relative level. As the sea cannot sink in one place without falling every where, Von Buch concluded that certain parts of Sweden and Finland were slowly and insensibly rising. Mr. Lyell, together with Von Hoff and others, still continued to entertain doubts with regard to the reality of this phenomenon, partly on grounds stated by former writers, and above enumerated, partly because Sweden and Norway have been, within the times of history, very free from violent earthquakes, and because the elevation was said to take place not suddenly and by starts, according to the analogy of the intermittent action of earthquakes and volcanoes, but slowly, constantly, and insensibly. Personal inspection, however, has led ~ Mr. Lyell to adopt Von Buch's conclusion. Having visited some parts of the shores of the Bothnian gulf, between Stockholm and Geffe, and of the W coast of Sweden, between Uddevalla and Gothenburg, districts particularly alluded to by Celsius, he examined several of the marks cut by the Swedish pilots, under the direction of the Swedish Academy of Sciences in 1820, and found the level of the B. in calm weather several inches below the marks. He also found the level of the waters several feet below marks made seventy or a hundred years before. He obtained similar results on the side of the ocean; and found in both districts that the testimony of the inhabitants agreed exactly with that of their ancestors recorded by Celsius. Mr. Lyell also confirms the accounts given by Von Buch of the occurrence on the side of the ocean, of elevated beds of recent shells at various heights, from 10 to 200 ft., and also discovered deposits on the side of the Bothnian gulf, between Stockholm and Gefle, containing fossil-shells of the same species which now characterize the brackish waters of that sea. These occur at various elevations, from 1 to 100 ft., and sometimes reach 50 m. inland. The shells are partly marine and partly fluviatile; the marine species are identical with those now living in the ocean, but are dwarfish in size, and never attain the average dimensions of those which live in waters sufficiently salt to enable them to reach their full development. Upon the whole, Mr. Lyell believes that certain parts of Sweden are undergoing a gradual rise to the amount of 2 or 3 ft. in a century, while other parts visited by him, farther to the S, appear to experience no movement. [Proceedings of the Brit. Assoc., 1834.] The waves of the B. do not swell so high as in the ocean; but they are more dangerous and harassing to shipping, as they succeed each other with greater rapidity and impetuosity; while its small depth, amounting on an average from 15 to 20 fath., but in many places hardly half so much,—the shallowness of the Prussian shore,—the rugged nature of the Swedish coasts,—and the sudden and frequent changes of the wind,—render this sea formidable to navigators.—Amber is deposited by it on the shores of Courland and Prussia; and it appears that all the knowledge which the Romans possessed of the B., was obtained from their merchants who journeyed by land to its shores in search of amber. Specific gravity and saltness.] The average quantity of salts held in solution by the waters of the ocean, says Professor T. Thomson, "amounts to about 1-27th of the weight of the water: that is to say, every 27 grains of sea-water contain one grain of these salts in solution. But this is very far from being the case with the water of the B. I took the sp. grav. of the sea-water of the frith of Forth,-of water taken up at Tunaberg on the coast of Södermanland, somewhat to the S of Stockholm,-of water taken up at the Sound, -and of water taken up off the Scaw point: the following were the results obtained. The sp. grav. were taken at the temperature of 60°. Specific gravity. "We see from the above table that the sp. grav. is least at Tunaberg, and that it gradually increases as we proceed towards the ocean. But even off the Scaw the sp. grav. is greatly inferior to that of the water in the frith of Forth, which I conceive to be the same with that of the German sea and of the Atlantic ocean in general. There is one circumstance, however, which I ought to mention. The water at Tunaberg, and at the Sound, was taken from the margin of the sea; for I had no means of going out to any distance from the shore. But as the places I pitched upon were far from the issue of any rivers, I conceive that this could not make any sensible difference. Besides, in both places, I had it in my power to go to the extremity of a pretty long pier, so that the water in both cases was in fact taken up at least 100 yards from the shore. The only other experiments made on the sp. grav. of the B., which I have had an opportunity of seeing, are those of Wilke, and as he does not tell us from what part of that sea his water was taken, they do not lead to any satisfactory conclusions. But it is probable, as he lived in Stockholm, that the part of the B. from which he drew his water would be as far N as that capital. He found that the sp. grav. was much influenced by the direction of the wind. The following are the results which he obtained: 1.0039 Wind at E. . . . Wind at W, . . . . . . 1.0067 Storm at W. . . . . . . . . 1.0118 1.0098 Wind at NW, . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . "From the great effect of the wind, one would be tempted to suspect that the place of the B. from which he took the water that he examined was pretty far to the S; but that does not agree with the first specific gravity which he found, and which is lower than that of the water taken up by me at Tunaberg. It is proper to state that I took up the water which I examined in calm weather, and there had been no violent winds for at least a month. So that I consider the results which I obtained, as yielding a pretty fair mean of the contents of the B. at the different places examined. "One thousand grains of each of these waters were evaporated to dryness at a temperature which never exceeded 180°, and the dry residue was kept for eight hours in the temp. of about 200°, by means of a steam-bath, and then weighed. The weights of the salt furnished by each specimen are as under: Weight of salt. Water of the frith of Forth, "From this table we see that the B. at Tunaberg scarcely contains one-fifth part of the saline contents of the ocean which surrounds Great Britain. The water at the Sound contains rather less than one third; and the difference between the water of the ocean and that at the Scaw, though less, is still very considerable. If we reckon the salt contained in the B., at Tunaberg, 1, the following table exhibits the relative proportion of salt contained in the water taken up at other places: Water from Tunaberg, . . . "The water at Tunaberg tastes slightly brackish, but is so little so that it may be drunk without much inconvenience. And I have been told by the sailors that they commonly use the water of the Baltic for their food. The water at the Sound is perceptibly salter, but still not disagreeably so. But the water off the Scaw tastes as salt as the water of the ocean itself. I analyzed a quantity of the salt obtained from the water taken up off the Scaw, by methods well known to chemists, and which it is unnecessary to detail here. The following was the result which I obtained. It may be considered as exhibiting the constituents of sea-water in general, for I presume it will hardly be doubted that the proportions are everywhere nearly the same: Common salt, "I have reason to believe that there exists likewise in sea-water a portion of sulphate of soda. I have not indeed been able to separate any such salt; but I find always a surplus of sulphuric acid which must be united to some alkaline body. Sulphate of lime is likewise present, but in so small a quantity, that in a general view of this kind it may be omitted altogether. Thus the B. sea differs entirely in its constitution from the water of the ocean,containing much less salt, and diminishing in saltness as we advance northward. If the same rate of diminution which I observed between the Sound and the Baltic, at Tunaberg, continue to the northern extremity of the gulf of Bothnia, the water at that part ought to be nearly as fresh as river water. "To what are we to ascribe this striking difference between the saltness of the B. and of the ocean? Was it originally a lake of fresh water, and did the sea break in upon it? Even supposing this the case, one would have expected that long ere now the saltness of the ocean would have diffused itself equably through the whole of the B. That it has not done so affords a striking proof of the extreme slowness with which substances diffuse themselves through water by chemical affinity. It shows also that the waters of different seas keep much more completely separate than could have been anticipated. The distance between the Scaw and the Sound does not exceed 120 m., yet the difference in the proportion of salt is enormous; the one containing nearly three times as much salt as the other. The striking difference between the saltness of various parts of the B., and between its saltness and that of the ocean, is rather hostile to the opinion of Dr. Halley, who conceived that all the salt in the sea has been originally carried into it by rivers, and left when that water was evaporated. According to this theory the sea was originally fresh, and it is becoming salter and salter every day. But if we consider the great difference between the saltness of neighbouring seas, and the different kind of saline ingredients which exist in some seas, —as the Dead sea,—we can hardly subscribe to the accuracy of this opinion. Neither is it likely that the sea was salt from the beginning, otherwise it would be impossible to account for the difference in the quantity of salt which exists in various seas, as this difference does not depend upon climate. For it appears from accurate experiments made, that the ocean is as salt in very high N latitudes, as it is under the equator. The number of rivers running into the B. is considerable, and some of those that traverse the countries of Russia and Poland are of great size. The most probable opinion, though it is not satisfactory, is to derive the saltness of the ocean from beds of rock-salt deposited at its bottom. On such a supposition we could account for the difference in the saltness of different parts of the ocean by the greater or less abundance of these salt beds. But the subject, for want of accurate knowledge of the saltness of inland seas, is not yet ripe for discussion." [Travels in Sweden.]-The B. has a very perceptible current; and when the wind blows strong from the N, the water becomes so fresh as to be even fit for drinking or preparing meat. Even in the hottest summers the B. is cooler than any other sea. Though it has no regular tides, being almost entirely surrounded by land, yet when a strong W wind prevails for any considerable time, its natural outlet is effected, and a large accession of water is forced into it from the North sea: on such occasions, it rises on its coasts a little above its ordinary level, perhaps to the extent of 2 or 3 ft. The ebbing and flowing of the German ocean, though very weak, is said to co-operate with the B., so that traces of their effects may be perceived. Heavy gales of wind are frequent in the B., particularly in the gulf of Finland; and it abounds likewise with rocks and shelves which render its navigation extremely hazardous. BAL Rivers.] The basin of this sea, or the surface it drains, is of great extent. Not fewer than 250 streams flow into the Baltic, on this side of the Frozen ocean, of which the principal are the Düna, the Oder, the Vistula, the Rega, the Persante, and the Niemen. Of these the Oder and the Vistula convey to the B. the drainage of regions upwards of 300 m. direct distance from its shores; while the Düna and the Niemen extend nearly an equal distance on the E. Perhaps the entire basin of the B. may be taken roughly at 550,000 sq. m. Islands.] The principal islands in this sea are Zealand, Rugen, Bornholm, Oland, Gothland, Dago, Esel, Cronstadt, Hochland, Tytersaari, Savansaari, Penisaari, Seitsaari, Mohn, Falster, and Aland. tant are salmon, pike, lampreys, streamlings. The streamlings are a degenerate species of herring, found on all the shores of the B., especially near Pernau, where they are sometimes taken in amazing quantities. The kyllo-streamling is a smaller and more delicate variety of that species, quite peculiar to these waters, and caught in great numbers in the autumnal season near Revel and Baltic-port. When pickelled, they make an excellent substitute for sardines and anchovies. The potted lampreys taken at Narva are equally delicate. Coasts.] The B. washes the coasts of Sweden, Russia, Denmark, Prussia, and Germany; and the productions of these countries form one of the most important branches of British commerce. The Russian ports in this sea-or more properly in the gulf of Finland-are Frederickshamm and Wyburg, in the gov. of Wynavigation to these ports is closed; and, as that season approaches, the weather becomes very tempestuous. Revel, Pernan, Arensburg, Hapsal, and Baltic-port, are shut up by the ice in November or December; and are generally open again in February or March. Riga is blocked up in October or November, and opens either in March or April. Narva, Cronstadt, Wyburg, and Frederickshamm, shut in October or November, and open in April; but at Petersburg the ice sometimes continues firm till May. The safest anchoring-places are Rogervyk bay or Baltic-port, Revelbay, Kasperwich under Hogland island, Aspo, and Sceskar. The coast is rocky and dangerous. All the ports, except Revel, are inconvenient and unsafe for loading and landing goods. By the aunexation of Courland to the imperial dominions, Russia gained the ports of Windau and Liebau.-Prussia possesses a territory of nearly 500 m. on the shore of the B., which, in that extent, receives some of the finest navigable rivers in Europe. There are likewise some branches of the B. which indent the Prussian coasts, and are called haffs, or lakes. Of these the largest is the Kurishhaff, which runs directly SW from Memel, spreading to a considerable breadth, and penetrating within 60 m, of Königsberg. The Frische-haff forms a long lake between Königsberg and Elbing, and communicates with the sea at Pillau; it is also connected with Dantzic by the branches of the Vistula. There is another haff in Pomerania, formed by the islands of Usedom and Wellin, and communicating with the B. by three channels, the principal of which is Swinemunde. The principal Prussian ports on the Baltic are Memel, Pillau, and Dantzic. With these ports Britain carries on a considerable trade. Timber is the chief article of exportation from Memel; corn and timber are the staple articles of the Dantzic trade.-Mecklenburg, a duchy in the circle of Lower Saxony, lying betwixt the B. and the Elbe, has only two ports on the B.,-Rostock and Wismar.-Sweden extends along the whole W coast of the B., on both sides of the gulf of Bothnia, and along part of the N coast of the gulf of Finland. It commands, of course, a considerable proportion of the B. trade. Its principal ports on that sea are Stockholm, Gottenburg, and Stralsund.—Though but a small part of the Danish dominions lies upon the B., yet that part is by far the most interesting; and has acquired importance from commerce. The duchies of Holstein and Sleswick, with Zealand, and some smaller islands, nearly shut up the communication between the B, and the ocean; and all the trade of Europe passes through that small country. Copenhagen, the capital of Zealand, and of the Danish dominions, is by far the most considerable port in this interesting territory: and next to it, in importance, is Elsinore, on the Sound.

BALTIMORE, a county in the N part of the state of Maryland, U. S., watered by Gunpowder and Patapsco rivers. The surface is hilly but not mountainous. Area 900 sq. m. The cap. is Baltimore. Pop, in 1830, 120,876; in 1840, 134,379, of whom 7,595 were slaves, and 21,453 free coloured.

BALTIMORE, the cap. of the above co., and the third city in respect of pop., and fifth in commerce, in the U.S., is situated on the N side of the Patapsco river, 14 m. above its entrance into Chesapeake bay, and 200 m. by ship-channel from the ocean, in N lat. 39° 17′ 13", W long. 76° 37′ 30". It is 40 m. NE of Washington; 97 m. SW of Philadelphia; and 183 m. SW of New York. The city is pleasantly situated on slightly undulat- of the foreign merchandise imported in 1849 was ing ground, with commanding elevations on the N and E. As laid out, it includes 4 sq. m., and is built around a bay which sets up from the N side of the Patapsco. The streets are regular and spacious, and the houses are neat, most of them of brick. The harbour, which is very fine, consists of three parts. The entrance to it, between Fort M'Henry and the Lazaretto, is about 600 yds. wide, with 22 ft. of water. This depth is continued, with an increased width, for a mile and a quarter, to near Fell's point. Opposite Fell's point, the width is contracted to one-fourth of a mile. This is the entrance to the second harbour, and is about 12 ft. deep; but it widens above into an ellipse, a mile long, half a mile broad, and 15 ft. deep. The third or inner harbour has a depth of 10 ft., and penetrates to near the centre of the city. It is well defended by Fort M'Henry, at the entrance to the outer harbour, which was proved by a powerful attack that was made upon it and repulsed, in the last war with Great Britain. Jones' falls, a small stream from the N, divides the city into two parts, and over it are erected 3 stone bridges and 4 wooden ones. Vessels of 500 or 600 tons can lie at the wharfs near Fell's point; but those of 200 tons can come up to the town in the inner harbour. The amount of the tonnage of this port in 1840, was 76,022.—Among the public buildings, the City hall, in Holliday-street, a plain building, having a portico with massive pillars, is occupied by the city council and several offices. The Court house is a large and commodious building, and is appropriated to the city and county courts, with their connected offices. It is 145 ft. long, and 65 ft wide, two stories high, There are six markets. The state penitentiary consists of three large buildings, besides workshops and constructed of marble and brick. In front are several Tuscan columns, and the whole is surmounted by a cupola of an imposing appearance. and some other buildings, and occupies 4 acres, containing gardens and walks surrounded by a stone wall 20 ft. high. The prisoners work together by day, and are confined in separate cells at night. The house-of-refuge is well fitted for its purpose. But the most imposing public structure is the Washington monument, at the intersection of Charles and Monument streets. Its base is 50 ft. square, and 20 ft. high; on which is a pedestal supporting a Doric column, 140 ft. high, on the top of which is a statue of Washington, 13 ft. high. The column is 20 ft. in diam. at the bottom, and 14 ft. at the top, and is ascended by a circular stair-case in the inside. The Battle-monument, erected in 1815, in commemoration of the successful defence of the city against an attack of the British in Sept. 1814, is 52 ft. high, including a statue on the top emblematical of the city. The names of those who fell in the battle are inscribed on the column in letters of gold.—B. is well supplied with pure and wholesome water. In several parts of the city are public springs or fountains, accessible to all the citizens. These fountains are enclosed by circular railings, and covered by small, open temples, consisting of columns supporting a dome. But a still more copious supply of water is obtained from an elevated part of Jones' falls, in an aqueduct half-a-mile long. The harbour is accessible through a great part of the year, though sometimes obstructed by ice. The commerce of B. is great, for which it enjoys peculiar facilities. It possesses most of the trade of Maryland, much of that of Western Pennsylvania, and a portion of that of the Western states. In its shipping, it is the fifth city in the union. It is the greatest market for tobacco in the U.S., and the greatest flour-market in the world. The total value cost 150,000 dollars; the almshouse, 375 ft. long, 5,761,159 dols., of which 5,255,218 dols. was imported in American vessels, and 505,941 dols. under foreign flags. The exports of domestic produce in American vessels amounted to 7,049,725 dols., and in foreign vessels to 1,374,401 dols.; whilst the value of foreign merchandise exported under the American flag was 121,470 dols., and under foreign flags, 144,084 dols. The total value of the exports was thus 8,689,680 dols. The total number of vessels which entered the port from foreign countries during the year was 496, <math>=</math> 112,502 tons, of which 359 vessels, <math>=</math> 84,620 tons, were American, and 137 vessels, = 27,882 tons, were foreign. The total clearances were 623, = 143,549 tons: of these 461, = 111,026 tons, were national, and 163, = 34,523 tons, were foreign. The total quantity of tobacco inspected during the year, as received from the several state tobacco warehouses, was 45,435 hhds., and the amount shipped during the year was 57,454 hhds. The returns furnished by six cotton factories show that the number of bales received by them was 8,219, whilst the number of yards turned out was 5,160,817. The real and personal property of the city is returned at 80,237,960 dols. for the year 1850: in 1849 it was 78,252,588 dols.; in 1848, 74,228,276 dols.; in 1847, 72,079,322 dols. Its manufactures are extensive.—The B. and Port-Deposite railroad extends 36 m. to Havre de Grace; and there connects with a chain of railroads to Philadelphia, making the whole distance 95 m.—The Washington branch of the B. and Ohio railroad extends 38 m. to Washington city.—The B. and Ohio railroad is completed over 80 miles to Harper's ferry, and is designed to be continued to Wheeling, on the Ohio. It already brings much trade into B., and when completed will form the most direct communication which exists between the Atlantic coast and the Mississippi valley. —The B. and Susquehanna railroad extends to York in Pennsylvania, and connects with a chain of railroads to Philadelphia and B.—There are lines of steam-packets to Philadelphia and to Norfolk, and other packets to New York and to various parts of the Atlantic coast. The university of Maryland was incorporated in 1812, and the B. college has since been merged in it. It has an academical and scientific, a medical and a law department. The medical dep. has 6 professors and 65 students.—St. Mary's college is a flourishing Catholic institution; has 25 instructors and 160 students, and 12,000 vols. in its libraries.- The Maryland institute has for its object the diffusion of scientific and mechanical knowledge, by means of lectures and otherwise. It has a fine chemical laboratory, and a complete philosophical apparatus.—The Maryland academy of science and literature, located in the Athenæum buildings, which were erected for scientific purposes, has collections in the several departments of natural history, plaster casts, &c.—Other educational institutions are Asbury college, and several free schools, founded by donations from benevolent individuals. There is a number of public schools, some of which have fine buildings, and are well provided with all the means of instruction. In 1840, it had 4 colleges with 395 students; 50 academies and grammar schools, with 2,044 students; 63 common schools with 3,401 scholars; and 1,191 white persons over twenty, who could neither read nor write.-There are in the city 42 churches, of which the Episcopalians have 5; the Roman Catholics 6, one of which is a splendid cathedral; the Presbyterians 3; the Scotch Presbyterians 2; the Baptists 4; the Methodists 9; and there are various others.—There are various benevolent institutions, among which are the hospital, the building of which with spacious grounds; several dispensaries and orphan-asylums, and some others. There are 2 theatres, a circus, and a museum, with some other places of amusement.—Baltimore was first laid out as a town in 1729, and in 1765 it contained but 50 houses. It received a charter as a city in 1797. Few towns in the U. S. have risen more rapidly; and its growth is of a substantial and permanent character.—The monthly mean temp. near B., calculated from 8 years' observation, from 1817 to 1824, was as follows: January The mean of all these observations is 52.94. During these 8 years the therm. sunk four times below zero.
BALTIMORE, a village in Fairfield co., Ohio, U. S., 32 m. SW of Columbus. Pop. 500.—Also a township in Windsor co., Vermont. Pop. 150.

BALTIMORE, an inlet of the sea, and a fine natural harbour, properly the estuary of the river Ilen, in co. Cork. It looks out upon an expanse of sea, which, over a distance of 7 m., is screened from W winds, and the swell of the Atlantic, by the islands of Cape Clear and Sherkin, and some intermediate rocks and islets; it enters by a strait or channel of about half-a-mile in breadth between a peninsula on the E and the island of Sherkin on the W; and its navigation is continued up the Ilen to Skibbereen, a distance of between 9 and 10 m. from the harbour's entrance, or of about 16 m. from the extremity of Cape Clear, the most southerly ground in Ireland. The harbour is free from any bar or other obstruction; its bosom is a mazy series of watery straits and expansions, intersected with island and promontory, -almost a labyrinth of sounds, coves, islets, and headlands. Were the fisheries of B. harbour judiciously managed, they might prove an inexhaustible source of wealth and plenty. In 1830, these fisheries employed 4 decked vessels of aggregately 107 tons; 27 half-decked vessels of 445 tons; 50 open sail-boats, and 336 row-boats, manned by 2,367 hands. In 1835, the exports were estimated in value at £37,144, and the imports were valued at £17,767. The customs collected at this port in 1829 amounted to £982; in 1836, to £2,151; and in 1841 had sunk to £960. The port embraces Castletownsend, Glendore, and Ross; with Bantry creek and Berehaven.-The v. and port of B., formerly a parliamentary borough in the p. of Tullagh, stands on the E shore of Baltimore harbour, 2 m. above its entrance, 5 m. NE by N of the nearest part of Cape Clear island, and 174 m. SW by S of Dublin. Pop. in 1831, 459; in 1841, 168. The O'Driscolls, long notorious for daring practices of piracy, were for several centuries the proprietors and occupants of B.; and some vestiges of their castle at the place are still to be seen.

BALTINGLASS, a parish, partly in co. Carlow, but chieffy in co. Wicklow. Area 6,383 acres,—of which 234 acres are in the Carlow section, and 60 acres in the town of B. Pop. in 1831, 4,110; in 1841, 4,436. The main head-stream and several early affluents of one river Slaney, trot out from the upland ravines; and flow southward along a luxuriant, embellished, and picturesque valley in this p., past the cheerful manufacturing v. of Stratford-upon-Slaney. The post and market-town of B., formerly a parl. borough, in the above p., stands 61 m. NW of Hacketstown, and 294 m. SSW of Dublin. Excepting the operations of two bleachfields, and the business of the weekly markets, little manufacture or trade is carried on. Pop. in 1831, 1,670; in 1841, 1,928. The family of Eustace, or Fitz-Eustace, were created Vis- BAL counts B. in 1541; and the family of Stratford, afterwards Earls of Aldborough, were created Barons of B. in 1763. BALTISKOI, BALTISCH, or BALTIC PORT, formerly called Roggerswick, or Roghervik, a town and port of Russia, in the gov. of Esthonia, and dist. of Revel, on the gulf of Finland, at the mouth of the river Paddes, 25 m. WSW of Revel, in N lat. 59° 20', E long. 24° 12'. Pop. 211. The port or bay is spacious; but, from its want of depth, incapable of receiving large vessels. Catherine II. conceived the plan of making this port the grand naval station of obliged her to renounce this idea. shire, 4 m. SE of Glastonbury. Area 2,700 acres. Pop. 718.

BALTOS, or Valtos, a small river of Greece, in the prov. of Acarnania, which takes its rise in the mountains to the E of the gulf of Arta, runs in a SSW direction, and, after uniting with the Voinikovo, falls into the Aspropotamo in N lat. 38° 43'.
BALTRAY, a village in the p. of Termonfeckan, co. Louth, on the N side of the Boyne. Pop. 423.

BALTRUM, a small island belonging to Hanover, near the N coast of E Friesland, in N lat. 53° 45', E long. 7° 24'. It is 7 m. in length, and 1½ m. in breadth; and is low, and liable to inundation in high tides. Its inhabitants, about 100 in number, are chiefly fishermen.

BALTSCHIK, or BALJIK, a town of Bulgaria, on the Black sea, at the mouth of a small stream 18 m. NE of Varna. The Tomi of the Romans, famous as the place of exile of Ovid, is supposed to have been in the neighbourhood of this place.
BALTZ, a town of Prussia, in Brandenburg, 30 m. NE of Frankfort.

BALU, or Bruze, an island of the Indian ocean, in the gulf of Martaban, separated by an arm of the Saluen from the coast of Pegu on the N, and of Siam on the E. It is about 20 m. in length, and 10 m. in breadth. Its chief towns are Goalut and Kumakay.

BALUDIN, or Bulwudun, a town of Turkey in Asia, between Akshehr and Afium-Karahissar, on the N side of a great plain at the foot of the Emirdagh. There are numerous remains of the time of the Byzantine empire here.—Leake.
BALUFFI MOTTA. See MOTTA BALUFFI.
BALUMBA, or BAULUMBA, a town of Hindostan, in the peninsula of Gujerat, near the embouchure of the Aji, in the gulf of Cutch.
BALUNDO. See BAILUNDO.

BALVAIG, a river of Perthshire, which, rising in the W skirts of Balquhidder p., flows ENE into Loch Doine, which it connects with Loch Voel, emerging from which it holds an E and then a S course to Loch Lubnaig, from the lower or S extremity of which it reissues, and flows SE to the Teith, which it joins at Bochastle, a little above Callander bridge.
BALVANJEITIE, a town of Hungary, on the Banat, SE of Pantschowa.
BALVANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicato, district of Potenza.
BALVE, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Westphalia, on the Kunne river, 8 m. SW of Arensberg. Pop. 800.
BALWIERZISKI, a town of Poland, in the waivodship of Augustow, at the junction of the Pierszaka with the Niemen, 22 m. E of Marianpol. The environs are fertile. Pop. 1,300.
BALWUNT, or BELWUNTA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa, in the dist. and 13 m. S of Cuttack, on the delta of the Mahanuddy.
BALZAC, a commune of France, in the dep. and on the l. bank of the Charente, cant. of Angouleme. Saffron in considerable quantities is cultivated in the | environs. Pop. 1,000.
BALZAS. See BALSAS.
BALZERS, a town in the principality of Liechtenstein, near the r. bank of the Rhine, and 5 m. N of Mavenfield.
BALZOLA, a commune and town of Piedmont, in the prov. of Alessandria, 4 m. NNW of Casale. Pop. 2,000.
BALZORANO, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo Ultra, 10 m. SE of Civitella-Roveto. Cattle are extensively reared in the environs. Pop. 1,200.

BAMBA, a town of Spain, in Leon, in the prov. and 8 m. NW of Valladolid. In a valley in the vicinity is a thermal spring called Foncaldo.—Also a town of Spain, in the prov. and 8 m. E of Zamora, on the l. bank of the Ojuelo, and near its junction with the Douro.—Also a town of Abyssinia, in the prov. and near the W bank of Lake Dembea, 50 m. SW of Gondar.

BAMBA, or Pemba, a district of Western Africa, the largest and richest in the kingdom of Congo, extending 120 m. along the coast of the Atlantic, from the Ambriz on the N, to the Luz on the S. It abounds in gold, silver, copper, iron, lead, and salt. The inhabitants are reputed the bravest in Congo. They are governed by a mani or prince. The cap., of the same name, is situated on a tributary of the Ambriz or Birge, in a fertile plain, 35 m. from the embouchure of that river.

BAMBAK, a district of Russia, in the gov. of Georgia, bounded on the N by the dist. of Somketia; on the E by that of Kazak; on the S by the dist. of Shuraguel; and on the W by the Persian prov. of Kars and Akalzik. It is intersected from W to E by a river of the same name, which takes its rise in the mountain-range which traverses the western portion of the district, winds to the NE, and uniting with the Gram and several other streams, falls into the Kur 30 m. SW of Tiflis.
BAMBAMANCA, a town of Peru, in the intendancy of Truxillo, 60 m. NW of Caxamarquilla.
BAMBAN POINT, a promontory of the island of Mindanao, in the Asiatic archipelago, forming the SE side of the entrance of Illana bay, in N lat. 6° 50', E long. 124°.
BAMBANGLO, a town on the W coast of the island of Luzon, in the Asiatic archipelago, in N lat. 18° 40′, E long. 120° 40′.

BAMBARAH, or Bumbhora, a ruined town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Sinde, on a woody hill near the southern base of the Garra mountains, and 17 m. E of Tattah. It is supposed to have been the city called by the Persians Brahminabad, Mahaura, or Manhawer, and described as a flourishing Hindu capital in the 10th century.

BAMBARRA, a kingdom of Western Africa, in Sudan, bounded on the N by Ludamar and the Sahara; on the NE by Masina, and on the E by Jinbala, which territories separate it from Timbuctu; on the S by Baidu and Wassulo; and on the W by Manding and Kaarta. It stretches from 11° 55' to 14° 30′ N lat.; and chiefly consists of a spacious and fertile plain traversed by the Niger. The SW portion of the territory is covered with ramifications of the great mountain-ranges of Kong; the other districts are generally level, and to a considerable extent fertile and well cultivated. It is intersected in a NE direction by the Joliba or Niger, which becomes navigable at Bammaku, the point where it enters B. In its course through B., it receives on the l. the Ba Bili, Ba-gai, or Frina; and on the r. the Bagoe or White river, which waters the SW region of the territory. The heat is very great, but not so intense as in some of the adjacent countries, and it is not gen- BAM erally insalubrious. The rainy season is from June to November, during which the prevailing winds are from the SW. At the commencement of the dry season the wind shifts to the NE.—The principal productions of the soil are rice, cassava, maize, millet, yams, and tobacco: neither the sugar-cane nor the coffee plant are known, and tropical fruits are but partially diffused. Cattle are scarce; but sheep and goats are numerous, and there is a good breed of horses. The woody banks of the rivers are infested with lions; and alligators exist in the rivers. Mines of gold and iron exist in some localities.—Although reputed to be inferior in industry to the Negroes of the Gold coast, the inhabitants of B., besides bestowing much attention upon agriculture, and cultivating cotton and indigo with success, have various manufactures of gold, iron, leather, and cotton. They make a species of beer from durrah and the lotus berries; and manufacture soap from the butter of the shea-tree, a species of bassia, which grows in great abundance. They also construct canoes by means of which they navigate the rivers of their territory. Their trade with the surrounding nations consists chiefly in slaves, gold from Bambuc, iron, cotton fabrics, ivory, shea-butter, and sometimes grain,—salt is their principal import.—The principal towns are Sego, the cap., with a pop. estimated by Park at 30,000, Sangsandi or Sansanding, and Jenne. The two first are situated on the Joliba, and the last on the White river.—The natives of Bambarra are Negroes of the Mandingo family, and speak a dialect of the language of that race. Fetichism is the religion of the state and of the mass of the people, but the Moors have established themselves in considerable numbers along the Joliba, and Mahommedanism is rapidly on the increase.—This kingdom, one of the most powerful and extensive of Nigritia, was almost entirely unknown to the Europeans previous to Park's discoveries, and our acquaintance with it is still extremely limited; but the pacific character of the inhabitants, and the favourable manner in which they have received overtures of friendly intercourse encourage the hope that its resources will ere long be made accessible to general commerce. — Park. — Caillié.
BAMBEAMOXIMA, a town of S Africa, in Benguela, near the l. bank of the Cutaco, 3 m. SE of Bihe.
BAMBECQUE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Hond-schoote. Pop. 1,139.

BAMBERG, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of the Upper Maine, beautifully situated on the Regnitz, 3 m. below its junction with the Maine, and 25 m. W of Bayreuth. It is partially surrounded by walls and ditches, and is divided by the river-which here branches into four streams—into three districts, which are connected by two handsome bridges, and the higher parts of which rise amphitheatrally on the acclivities of the hills which bound the landscape on the E. The principal streets are long and of proportionate breadth, and are well-paved and lighted. The ditches, and, to some extent, the ramparts, have been converted into walks and gardens. The chief buildings are the cathedral, a handsome edifice with four towers, containing numerous monuments and paintings by the most eminent masters, rebuilt by Bishop Otho in the 11th cent.; the old imperial and episcopal palace, now a guard-house; the modern prince-episcopal palace, still unfinished; 16 churches, of which St. Martin's and St. James's -especially the former - are remarkable for the beauty and simplicity of their architecture; 15 chapels, several convents, the abbey of St. Michaelsberg, situated on the eminence from which it takes its name, and commanding a magnificent prospect, two colleges, with a library containing 52,000 vols., and a rich collection of scarce manuscripts, a museum, a picture-gallery, &c. There are also a polytechnical school, a gymnasium, a lyceum, an ecclesiastical seminary, a normal school, a medical school, an hospital, a workhouse, a lazzaretto, &c. The townhall, formerly a Carmelite convent, stands on an island of the Regnitz. The chief manufactures of B. are cloth, calico, leather, tobacco, porcelain, starch, sealing-wax, gloves, gold and silver plate, marble - ornaments, musical instruments, and carriages, &c. There are also oil, corn and tan mills, wine-presses, 4 printing establishments, and numerous breweries. Boat-building, and the cultivation of gardens and of nursery-grounds, form also important branches of the industry of this town. The commerce, which is extremely active, consists chiefly in grain, beer, fruit, liquorice of which 50,000 lbs. of local produce are annually exported, flowers, plants, and timber. The facilities for communication which its situation commands, enhanced as these now are by means of the canal between the Maine and the Danube, render this town one of the most important commercial entrepots in the kingdom. It has two annual fairs. B. is of considerable antiquity. Its university, founded in 1647, was suppressed in 1803. It is now the seat of a catholic archbishop, metropolitan of the dioceses of Eichstadt, Wurtzbourg, and Spire. Pop. in 1831, 18,600, of whom 16,820 were Catholics, 1,380 Protestants, and 400 Jews; in 1837, 4,388 families and 21,251 individuals. The circle of Bamberg, situated in one of the richest and most beautiful districts of Central Germany, comprises an area of 164 sq. m. It is divided into two bailiwicks, and besides the town of the same name, contains 43 villages and hamlets, and a total pop. of 31,000. It was formerly a rich and populous principality, and constituted an independent bishopric founded in 1007. The latter was secularised by the prince-bishop Luneville; and in 1803 the former was assigned by a decree of the German empire to Bavaria.
BAMBERG, or New Baumberg, a town of Hesse- Darmstadt, 20 m. SW of Maynz, near the l. bank of the Appel.
BAMBIDERSTROFF, a commune of France, in the dep. of Moselle, cant. of Fauquemont. Pop. 969.

BAMBROUGH, or BAMBOROUGH, a ward and p. in Northumberland. The ward is bounded on the N by Islandshire; on the W by Glendale and Coquetdale wards; on the S by the Aln; and on the E by the German ocean. It is about 17 m. in length, and 8 m. in breadth, and consists principally of arable ground. It contains 7 parishes. Area in British statute acres 69,650. Pop. in 1831, 10,842; in 1841, 10,944. The ward comprises the p. of B., part of Belford, Ellingham, Embleton, Long Houghton, Howick, and Lesbury.—The p. of B. comprises the chapelries of Beadnell and Lucker, and the townships of Adderstone, Bambrough, Bambrough-castle, Bradford, Budle, Burton, Elford, Fleetham, Glororum, Hoppen, Mouson, Newham, Newstead, Outchester, Ratchwood, Shoston, Spindleston, North Sunderland, Swinhoe, Tughal, Warenton, and Warnford. Pop. in 1831, 3,949; in 1841, 4,237. Area 25,100 acres.—The village of B. is situated on the coast, 5 m. E by N of Belford. It was anciently a royal burgh and a market-town called Bebbanburg, and gave name to an extensive district called Bambroughshire, possessed of certain privileges now obsolete. Pop. in 1841, 375.

BAMBROUGH-CASTLE, a township in the p. and ward of Bambrough, 5 m. E of Belford; named from its vicinity to the celebrated castle of B., which stands on a high and rugged triangular rock of basalt, projecting into the sea, accessible only from the SE side, and rising 150 ft. above the water-mark. BAM This castle and the manor belonging to it, on the forfeiture of Thomas Foster, in 1715, was purchased by his uncle Lord Crewe, bishop of Durham, and, with other considerable estates, left vested in trustees, to be applied to charitable uses. From the top of the tower, signals are made to the fishermen of Holy Island, when any vessel is discovered in distress. In the castle there are also an infirmary and dispensary, with a resident surgeon, and an extensive collection of theological books, founded by Lord Crewe's trustees, in 1778, and open to every respectable householder within 20 m. of B., and every clergyman of whatever denomination. Pop. in 1801, 60; in 1841, 59.
BAMBOS, or BAMBUS BERG, a mountain ridge in S Africa, to the NE of the dist. of Tarka, in S lat. 31° 25′, and E long. 26° 25′.

BAMBUK, a kingdom of W Africa, in Senegambia; bounded on the N by Kajaaga; on the NE by Kasson, from which, and from Logo-Bruko on the E, it is separated by the Senegal; on the S by Konkodu and Satadu; and on the W by Bondu, from which it is separated by the Faleme. It extends between 12° and 14° N lat., and between 8° and 11° W long. It is extremely mountainous; and is intersected in a N direction by numerous streams, tributaries of the Senegal. Its SW districts are watered by the Sanon- Kolez or Gold river, which unites with the Faleme 35 m. to the S of the confluence of that river with the Senegal. The higher summits are bare and arid, but the soil of the lower lands is of the richest description, producing maize, rice, millet, water-melons, leguminous plants, &c., and the richest varieties of tropical vegetation almost without the aid of culture. Cattle in immense numbers, horses, sheep, goats and camels are to be met with in all its districts, and elephants are common in the plains: but the most remarkable of the aboriginal animals of this territory are the white ass,—of which the inhabitants make a strict monopoly—the giraffe, and the white fox. Bees are also abundant. Iron, silver, lead, tin, and loadstone are amongst its mineral riches, but the staple produce of the kingdom is gold, the beds of which are perhaps the richest in the world. It is found in alluvial districts of Bakkow, Semayla, Hambia, and Hombadyria, and is separated from the earth with which it is commingled by washing. At Natakon 80 lbs. of alluvium yield an average produce of 144 grains; and at Semayla the deposit is considerably greater. Cotton fabrics, and a few other species of manufactures and salt, especially the latter, are the chief articles of import. The inhabitants of B., estimated at 60,000, belong to the great Mandingo family. The government is a limited monarchy, the chiefs of each village possessing an hereditary jurisdiction. The prevailing religion is Mahommedanism. A colony was early formed by the Portuguese in this territory, and for 300 years its existence remained unknown to the rest of Europe. It was ultimately exterminated by the natives. The chief town, of the same name, is situated near the source of the Gold river.
BAMBRUGGE, a village of Holland, in the prov. and 13 m. NW of Utrecht, on the Kromme-Amstel, Pop. 900.
